title: How To Train Your Researcher
text: Click here if you are not redirected automatically.

title: Omniscient Reader’s Viewpoint (ORV)
text:
Reading research papers is a skill — it takes deliberate practice and good habits. This guide compiles advice from graduate students and professors on how to approach AI research papers and academic articles efficiently and critically.






Note



This isn’t a page that tells you “how to read a research paper”. It’s more of a “you can try these tips because it has worked for us” kind of page. Learning when to skim and when to read deeply is part of the skill which you should apply to this page as well!






Know why you are reading. Are you looking for related work, a specific method, or a broad survey of a field / problem (e.g., AI hallucinations)? Your goal determines how broad or deep you go.
Check the venue. NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, EMNLP — knowing the conference gives you a sense of the paper’s quality and focus area.
Read the abstract twice. Once to understand the claim; once to identify what you should be looking for in the body.

title: Omniscient Reader’s Viewpoint (ORV)
text:
Know why you are reading. Are you looking for related work, a specific method, or a broad survey of a field / problem (e.g., AI hallucinations)? Your goal determines how broad or deep you go.
Check the venue. NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, EMNLP — knowing the conference gives you a sense of the paper’s quality and focus area.

title: Omniscient Reader’s Viewpoint (ORV)
text:
Critical Questions to Ask

What is the core problem being solved?
What is the key insight or novelty?
What assumptions does the method rely on?
How strong is the experimental validation?
What are the limitations the authors acknowledge — and those they don’t?

title: Omniscient Reader’s Viewpoint (ORV)
text:
Starting Points
Part of my research journey was figuring out which conferences are considered “reputable” and/or “impactful”. While this list is non-exhaustive, I listed some of them here to help you (hopefully an aspiring AI researcher) find a starting point.








Conference
Full Name
Primary Focus & Specializations




NeurIPS
Neural Information Processing Systems
Machine learning: deep learning architectures; reinforcement learning; theoretical foundations.


ICML
International Conference on Machine Learning
Algorithmic machine learning: optimization techniques; statistical learning theory; generative modeling.


ICLR
International Conference on Learning Representations
Representation learning: deep neural network optimization; self-supervised learning; geometric structures.


CVPR
Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition
Computer vision: image recognition and synthesis; video analysis; multimodal vision-language models.


ACL
Association for Computational Linguistics
Natural language processing: computational linguistics; large language model alignment; text generation.


KDD
Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining
Data mining: large-scale knowledge discovery; graph neural networks; industrial applications.


SIGIR
Special Interest Group on Information Retrieval
Information retrieval: search mechanics; recommendation systems; web text mining.


AAAI
Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence
General artificial intelligence: knowledge representation; multi-agent systems; heuristic search.









Note



AI is often cross disciplinary! If you are working on such projects, please do your homework to find out which other venues — both conferences and journals — are considered strong/impactful for your field.

Introduction
Diagrams are really important. A good diagram quickly conveys the high-level idea of your approach to readers before they dig deep into the methodology of the papoer. When people read your paper, they will usually look at your abstract, introduction, tables, and your main diagram before they dig deeper into the details of your methodology.
Diagramming style heavily depends on the venue that you try to publish at. For example, in clinical venues, the diagram should emphasise how clinicians or medical end users can make use of your contribution (e.g. dataset, new model architecture) in their real-world situations. (pls add more egs). You should always look at the diagrams of previous successful papers published at your target venue to understand aspects like how detailed your diagram should be, what visual style reviewers like, and how much explanation you should include in your diagram.


What your diagram should and shouldn’t do, and some general tips

Your diagram is not just a repetition of whatever your wrote in the text. Your diagram should be able to convey how your method works faster than yours readers reading through your text description of the method
Your diagram should make your contribution extremely obvious. The reader should be able to explain the high-level idea of what your contribution is after looking at your diagram.
The flow of information should be obvious. All entities (e.g. boxes, arrows, losses, prediction results) should be labelled clearly
Do not waste time and space explaining standard components (e.g. softmax, binary cross-entropy). Reviewers are experienced and already know this stuff.
Using visual language can be helpful (for example, your can use green to denote positive model behaviour and red for undesirable model behaviour)



Types of diagrams
The type of diagram usually is tied to the type of contribution it is representing. Broadly speaking, diagrams usually fall under one of these types (is there really a point to this since technically there are as many types of diagrams as there are contributions?) 1. Dataset creation pipeline 2. New model architecture 3. New loss function or training objective diagram 4. New task formulation diagram 5.

Introduction
Experiments are literally the building blocks of research. In each weekly or daily iteration, you will normally run experiments to test whether you idea works, understand why your idea succeeds or fails, or decide what you want to explore next.
Negative experimental results that challenge your hypothesis are not inherently bad, and are not a reflection of your ability as a researcher. A negative result just tells you that your idea does not behave as expected. If your experiment has a good design, you will be able to narrow down what beliefs are true, what beliefs are false, and what you should investigate next.
Every experiment, or set of experiments that you run, should answer a question that you have.


How to plan a useful experiment

Define what are you trying to do (e.g. hyperparameter tuning, finding out the effect of some new submodule you designed)
Define your baseline/control (configuration you compare with to isolate the effect of your change)
Define what variables you are going to change, and what values are you going to set these variables
Define what metrics or qualitative examples that will help you answer your question. For example: Performance metrics lik F1, qualitative visualisations like Grad-CAM that explain model predictions
Describe the results you expect to see, and what you do not expect to see
After viewing the results of your experiment, figure out the list of facts that you can conclude from the experiment.
Interpret your results to extract some insights, and then you can make an action plan based on your insights



Experiment Hygiene

Do not change more than one variable at a time. This will make it diffciult for you to isolate the effect of each component you have changed
Keep a clear baseline. Each experiment that you run should have a control setup that you are comparing to
Record the exact configuration of your experiment. There will definitely be points in time where you want to reproduce a result that you got from some experiment and regret not recording said experiment configuration
Failed experiments are important and you should record them too
Facts are not the same as interpreptation. For example, if

title: How To Train Your Researcher (HTTYR)
text:
This page is specific to the Early Research Opportunity Program (EROP) at SUTD. It walks mentors and students through the “I Do, We Do, You Do” teaching strategy — a scaffolded approach that progressively transfers responsibility from mentor to student.






Note



These guides are living documents. They are meant to reduce friction, not to be treated as the definitive rulebook for doing research.

title: How To Train Your Researcher (HTTYR)
text:
This page is specific to the Early Research Opportunity Program (EROP) at SUTD. It walks mentors and students through the “I Do, We Do, You Do” teaching strategy — a scaffolded approach that progressively transfers responsibility from mentor to student.






Note



These guides are living documents. They are meant to reduce friction, not to be treated as the definitive rulebook for doing research.

title: How To Train Your Researcher (HTTYR)
section: The “I Do, We Do, You Do” Framework
text:
The “I Do, We Do, You Do” Framework
The framework breaks mentorship into three phases of decreasing mentor involvement and increasing student autonomy.

I Do — Modelling
The mentor demonstrates the skill or process while thinking aloud. Students observe and ask questions.
Example: > The mentor reads an AI research paper aloud, narrating the decisions they make > as they skim the abstract, identify the key contribution, and assess > experimental validity.


We Do — Guided Practice
The mentor and student work through a task together. The student takes the lead; the mentor provides real-time prompts and feedback.
Example: > The student reads a new paper while the mentor asks guiding questions: > “What problem is this solving?”, “What baseline did they compare against?”, > “Would this method work in a different domain?”


You Do — Independent Practice
The student completes the task independently and presents their work. The mentor reviews and gives structured feedback afterward.
Example: > The student independently reads and summarises three papers from a reading list, > then presents a brief synthesis to the mentor in the next meeting.

title: SSS Class Content Creator (3SC)
text:
Academic writing is built from small, repeatable techniques. This page is a collection of these techniques (collectively known as writing advice) that an aspiring research might find useful. We hope that the contents here would be useful for you (the aspiring researcher) in becoming more aware of these sentence/paragraph-level patterns and to use them effectively for structuring arguments, signal relationships between ideas, and guiding readers through your article.






Note



These are techniques, not rules. Learn the pattern first, then develop your own voice around it.

title: SSS Class Content Creator (3SC)
text:
Topic Sentences
Every paragraph should open with a topic sentence that states the paragraph’s single claim. The rest of the paragraph supports it.
Weak opening (announces the topic, not a claim): > “This section discusses the limitations of our approach.”
Strong opening (makes a claim): > “Our approach has three limitations that constrain its applicability to > low-resource settings.”

title: SSS Class Content Creator (3SC)
